# List Workflow Runs via MagicBell CLI

## Background

MagicBell workflows orchestrate multi-step notification campaigns. To monitor or debug these campaigns, you need to retrieve their execution history using the MagicBell CLI.

## Requirements

* Write a bash script `list_runs.sh` that lists the runs for a specific workflow key.
* The workflow key will be passed as the first argument to the script.
* The script must use the MagicBell CLI to fetch the runs.
* Ensure the CLI is authenticated non-interactively using the provided environment variables (`MAGICBELL_EMAIL`, `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN`, `MAGICBELL_API_KEY`, `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`).
* Output the raw result of the CLI command to stdout.

## Implementation Hints

* Install `magicbell-cli` globally via npm.
* Authenticate the CLI using `magicbell login --manual` with the provided environment variables.
* Use the `magicbell workflow list_runs` command, passing the workflow key via the `--workflow_key` flag.
* The workflow key is available as `$1` in the bash script.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Command: bash list\_runs.sh \<workflow\_key>
* The stdout should print the CLI's output for the workflow runs.

**Confirming Write Permissions**

I'm verifying that `/home/user/myproject` exists and is writable. The `listFiles` output confirming an empty directory suggests I can proceed with writing my script there, specifically at `/home/user/myproject/list`.

Verify the script works or is correct

content.text:
# CLI - MagicBell
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-02, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/docs/libraries/magicbell-cli)

The MagicBell CLI provides two ways to authenticate: interactive browser-based authentication and manual authentication with API keys.
Interactive authentication is recommended for local development, while manual authentication is better suited for CI/CD environments.
...
Log in and authenticate your MagicBell user account to generate a set of authentication keys.
...
If the browser doesn't open automatically, please visit:
https://api.magicbell.com/cli/auth/initiate?…
...
Optionally, if you don't want to use a browser, use the `--manual` flag to authenticate with existing keys.
This can be helpful when authenticating the CLI in CI/CD environments.
This option requires your email, project's JWT, API Key and Secret Key, which are available in the MagicBell Dashboard.
```
magicbell login \
--manual \
--email $EMAIL \
--jwt $JWT \
--api-key $API_KEY \
--secret-key $SECRET_KEY
```

# Github workflow_dispatch Event Notification - MagicBell
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-06-02, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/workflows/github/workflow-dispatch)

Connect Github to MagicBell to receive events and trigger workflows.
This guide uses the MagicBell CLI.

# Github push Event Notification - MagicBell
created: 2025-01-15, last updated: 2026-03-31, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/workflows/github/push)

Connect Github to MagicBell to receive events and trigger workflows.
This guide uses the MagicBell CLI.
...
Use the MagicBell CLI to save this workflow to your project.
You can also use the Workflows API endpoint instead.
```
magicbell workflow save \
--data '{"key":"integration.github.push","steps":[{"command":"broadcast","input":{"action_url":"{{payload.compare}}","content":"{{payload.pusher.name}} pushed to {{payload.ref}}: {{payload.head_commit.message}}","overrides":{"providers":{"slack":{}}},"recipients":[{"external_id":"engineering-team"}],"title":"Push to {{payload.repository.full_name}}"}}]}'
```
...
Use the GitHub CLI to forward webhook events from your repository to MagicBell:
...
gh webhook forward \
...
Check that MagicBell received the event and executed the workflow:
```
magicbell workflow list_runs --workflow_key integration.github.push
```

# Expo Push Notification Guide - MagicBell
created: 2026-03-03, last updated: 2026-06-01, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/blog/a-guide-to-push-notifications-on-expo)

The guide also uses the MagicBell CLI to send broadcasts from the command line.
It will come in handy during testing, so make sure to install it.
...
Now all you need is some notifications to show.
You can create them from your command line using the MagicBell CLI:
- magicbell login
- magicbell broadcasts create –recipients [USER-EMAIL] –title “Notifications for everybody” –content “This call could have been a notification” (Don’t forget to insert your user’s email)

# Github status Event Notification
created: 2025-01-16, last updated: 2026-05-29, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/workflows/github/status)

Connect Github to MagicBell to receive events and trigger workflows.
This guide uses the MagicBell CLI.
...
Use the MagicBell CLI to save this workflow to your project.
You can also use the Workflows API endpoint instead.
```
magicbell workflow save \
...
Use the GitHub CLI to forward webhook events from your repository to MagicBell:
```
gh webhook forward \
--events=status \
--url=https://api.magicbell.com/v2/integrations/github/webhooks/incoming/{id}
```
...
```
magicbell workflow list_runs --workflow_key integration.github.status
```

# Stripe checkout.session.completed Event Notification - MagicBell
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-31, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/workflows/stripe/checkout-session-completed)

Connect Stripe to MagicBell to receive events and trigger workflows.
This guide uses the MagicBell CLI.
...
Copy the ID from the response and use it to build your webhook URL:
`https://api.magicbell.com/v2/integrations/stripe/webhooks/incoming/{id}`
…
...
Use the MagicBell CLI to save this workflow to your project.
You can also use the Workflows API endpoint instead.
```
magicbell workflow save \
--data '{"key":"integration.stripe.checkout.session.completed","steps":[{"command":"broadcast","input":{"action_url":"{{payload.data.object.success_url}}","content":"Thank you for your purchase! Your order of ${{payload.data.object.amount_total}} has been confirmed.","recipients":[{"external_id":"{{payload.data.object.customer}}"}],"title":"Order confirmed"}}]}'
```
...
### 1.
Trigger a test event
Use the Stripe CLI to send a test event:
```
stripe trigger checkout.session.completed
```
...
Check that MagicBell received the event and executed the workflow:
```
magicbell workflow list_runs --workflow_key integration.stripe.checkout.session.completed
```
...
```
magicbell workflow fetch_run --run_id {run_id}
```

# MagicBell - meijer.works
created: undefined, last updated: 2026-05-30, [Read more](https://meijer.works/projects/magicbell/)

- **SDK Development**: Created multiple JavaScript SDKs to streamline integration for developers, including:
- **`magicbell`**: The core JavaScript API client.
- **`@magicbell/cli`**: A CLI tool for developers.

# @magicbell/cli
created: 2023-10-09, last updated: 2025-03-31, [Read more](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@magicbell/cli/v/2.2.3?activeTab=versions)

This package provides a convenient interface to query the MagicBell API.
...
Node 18 or higher.
...
Install the package with npm:
npm install -g @magicbell/cli
or yarn:
yarn global add @magicbell/cli
or brew:
brew tap magicbell-io/magicbell brew install magicbell-cli
## Usage
The cli needs to be configured with your project's api key and api secret key, which are available in the MagicBell Dashboard.
Please run
`magicbell login` to set up your credentials.

# Github branch_protection_rule Event Notification - MagicBell
created: 2000-01-01, last updated: 2025-11-28, [Read more](https://www.magicbell.com/workflows/github/branch-protection-rule)

Connect Github to MagicBell to receive events and trigger workflows.
This guide uses the MagicBell CLI.
...
Copy the ID from the response and use it to build your webhook URL:
`https://api.magicbell.com/v2/integrations/github/webhooks/incoming/{id}`
...
Use the MagicBell CLI to save this workflow to your project.
You can also use the Workflows API endpoint instead.
```
magicbell workflow save \
--data '{"key":"integration.github.branch_protection_rule","steps":[{"command":"broadcast","input":{"content":"Branch protection rule for {{payload.rule.name}} was {{payload.action}} in {{payload.repository.full_name}} by {{payload.sender.login}}.","overrides":{"providers":{"slack":{}}},"recipients":[{"external_id":"security-team"}],"title":"Branch protection {{payload.action}}: {{payload.rule.name}}"}}]}'

**Validating Authentication Credentials**

I'm currently focusing on validating the provided authentication credentials. The bash command snippet looks promising for establishing a connection.

**Confirming Authentication Logic**

I'm now considering how to best handle non-interactive authentication for the script. It seems the `magicbell login` command typically stores credentials, but for a robust script, I need to decide whether to execute `magicbell login` within the script or if I should check for existing authentication first. My current focus is on ensuring it works seamlessly across different environments.

**Verifying Script Inputs**

I'm now re-evaluating how the workflow key is passed into the script, confirming it's the first argument as expected. {"errors":[{"code":"invalid_jwt_format","message":"expected authorization header format: Bearer <token>","suggestion":"Please provide a valid JWT in the 'Authorization' header","help_link":"https://www.magicbell.com/docs/api/authentication"}]}

part.text:
**Processing Command Output**

I've realized the `magicbell workflow list_runs` command outputs JSON, but `magicbell login` outputs plain text. To keep stdout clean for JSON parsing, I'm considering redirecting the login command's output to stderr.

**Redirecting Login Output**

I've decided to redirect the `magicbell login` command's output to stderr using `>&2`. This ensures stdout remains exclusively for the JSON output of `magicbell workflow list_runs`, guaranteeing clean parsing for downstream processes. Error messages from login will still be visible.

todos.content: Research MagicBell CLI login and workflow list_runs commands
Verify the script works or is correct

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash

# Exit immediately if a command exits with a non-zero status
set -e

# Make sure the workflow key is provided
if [ -z "$1" ]; then
  echo "Error: workflow key is required." >&2
  echo "Usage: bash list_runs.sh <workflow_key>" >&2
  exit 1
fi

WORKFLOW_KEY="$1"

# Authenticate the MagicBell CLI non-interactively
# Redirect stdout of login to stderr to keep stdout clean for the workflow runs output
magicbell login \
  --manual \
  --email "$MAGICBELL_EMAIL" \
  --jwt "$MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN" \
  --api-key "$MAGICBELL_API_KEY" \
  --secret-key "$MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY" >&2

# Fetch and output the raw workflow runs to stdout
magicbell workflow list_runs --workflow_key "$WORKFLOW_KEY"

input.command: MAGICBELL_EMAIL="test@example.com" MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN="dummy" MAGICBELL_API_KEY="dummy" MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY="dummy" bash /home/user/myproject/list_runs.sh my-workflow-key

#!/usr/bin/env bash

# Exit immediately if a command exits with a non-zero status
set -e

# Make sure the workflow key is provided
if [ -z "$1" ]; then
  echo "Error: workflow key is required." >&2
  echo "Usage: bash list_runs.sh <workflow_key>" >&2
  exit 1
fi

WORKFLOW_KEY="$1"

# Authenticate the MagicBell CLI non-interactively
# Redirect stdout of login to stderr to keep stdout clean for the workflow runs output
magicbell login \
  --manual \
  --email "$MAGICBELL_EMAIL" \
  --jwt "$MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN" \
  --api-key "$MAGICBELL_API_KEY" \
  --secret-key "$MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY" >&2

# Fetch and output the raw workflow runs to stdout
magicbell workflow list_runs --workflow_key "$WORKFLOW_KEY"

part.text:

input.result:
I have written the `list_runs.sh` script to list workflow runs via the MagicBell CLI.

### Summary of Actions
1. **Installed CLI**: Installed `magicbell-cli` globally as specified in the hints.
2. **Created Script**: Created the bash script `/home/user/myproject/list_runs.sh`.
3. **Non-Interactive Authentication**: Configured the script to authenticate the MagicBell CLI using `magicbell login --manual` with the provided environment variables:
   - `MAGICBELL_EMAIL`
   - `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN`
   - `MAGICBELL_API_KEY`
   - `MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY`
4. **Clean Output**: Redirected the login command's stdout to stderr to ensure that the stdout of `list_runs.sh` contains only the clean, raw JSON output of the workflow runs.

### Script Details
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

# Exit immediately if a command exits with a non-zero status
set -e

# Make sure the workflow key is provided
if [ -z "$1" ]; then
  echo "Error: workflow key is required." >&2
  echo "Usage: bash list_runs.sh <workflow_key>" >&2
  exit 1
fi

WORKFLOW_KEY="$1"

# Authenticate the MagicBell CLI non-interactively
# Redirect stdout of login to stderr to keep stdout clean for the workflow runs output
magicbell login \
  --manual \
  --email "$MAGICBELL_EMAIL" \
  --jwt "$MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN" \
  --api-key "$MAGICBELL_API_KEY" \
  --secret-key "$MAGICBELL_SECRET_KEY" >&2

# Fetch and output the raw workflow runs to stdout
magicbell workflow list_runs --workflow_key "$WORKFLOW_KEY"
```
